name: math
description: Boardroom-quality math layouts for mathematicians, quants, ML researchers, physicists, statisticians, and economists. KaTeX-rendered equations with persona-appropriate surround.
purpose: Use when the slide IS the equation. KaTeX renders `$$тАж$$` as centred display blocks and `$тАж$` inline. Variants surround the math with the structure each persona expects: hero + legend (feature), step + justification (derivation), Definition/Theorem/Proof cards (theorem), side-by-side comparison (compare), equation + plot (canvas), matrix + properties (matrix), estimate ┬▒ uncertainty + interpretation (stats).

eyebrow.description: Optional rubric label above the heading (e.g. 'Linear regression ┬╖ OLS').

heading.description: One-sentence framing of what the math establishes.

equation.description: Display equation wrapped in `$$тАж$$`. KaTeX renders centred.

legend.description: 'where:' legend. Each li introduces an `$x$` symbol followed by its definition.

skeleton:
<!-- _class: math -->

### Eyebrow ┬╖ context

## One-sentence framing of what the equation establishes.

$$ y = f(x) $$

- $y$ тАФ what we predict
- $x$ тАФ input variable
- $f$ тАФ the relation under study
sample:
<!-- _class: math -->

### Linear regression ┬╖ OLS

## The closed-form estimator.

$$ \hat\beta = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y $$

- $\hat\beta$ тАФ OLS coefficient vector
- $X$ тАФ design matrix, $n \times p$
- $y$ тАФ response vector, length $n$
- $X^\top X$ тАФ Gram matrix, $p \times p$, must be invertible

whenToUse.title: The equation IS the argument
whenToUse.body: When a single closed-form expression, identity, or estimator carries the slide. KaTeX renders it; Lattice gives it the room. For surrounding prose with one inline `$x$`, use content.

whenToUse.title: Pick the variant from the persona
whenToUse.body: Quants reach for feature and stats. ML researchers reach for canvas (equation + plot). Pure mathematicians reach for theorem and derivation. Linear-algebra-heavy work reaches for matrix. The base layout works for everyone.

whenToUse.title: Legend, not footnotes
whenToUse.body: The `where:` list under the equation defines every symbol introduced. The audience should never have to scroll back to remember what $\hat\beta$ or $X$ stands for.

antiPatterns.title: Two display equations in the base layout
antiPatterns.body: The bare math layout is built around one hero equation. For side-by-side display, use `math compare`. For a derivation chain, use `math derivation`. Stacking two `$$` blocks in the base layout breaks the visual contract.

antiPatterns.title: Symbols without a legend
antiPatterns.body: An equation with three undefined symbols is a puzzle, not a claim. Either every non-trivial symbol gets a legend entry, or the equation is simple enough that the audience knows it cold.

antiPatterns.title: ASCII math instead of KaTeX
antiPatterns.body: Writing `beta_hat = (X'X)^-1 X'y` as plain text bypasses the renderer. Always wrap math in `$$тАж$$` (display) or `$тАж$` (inline) тАФ KaTeX is the entire reason this layout exists.

related.name: code
related.when: the implementation, not the equation, is the argument

related.name: diagram
related.when: the structure of the model, not its closed form

related.name: stats
related.when: a row of statistical results without a single equation focus

related.name: content
related.when: one inline equation inside a paragraph of prose

feature.label: Feature тАФ hero equation + legend
feature.caption: Alias for the base layout тАФ eyebrow, headline, hero equation, legend. Use when you want to make the persona framing explicit in the class list.
feature.sample:
<!-- _class: math feature -->

### Logistic regression ┬╖ MLE

## The log-likelihood we maximise.

$$ \ell(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \log \sigma(x_i^\top \beta) + (1 - y_i) \log\bigl(1 - \sigma(x_i^\top \beta)\bigr) \right] $$

- $\ell$ тАФ log-likelihood, concave in $\beta$
- $\sigma$ тАФ logistic link, $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$
- $y_i$ тАФ observed label, $\in \{0,1\}$
- $x_i$ тАФ feature vector for observation $i$

derivation.label: Derivation тАФ proof chain with justification column
derivation.caption: Two-column table: derivation steps on the left, the justification for each step on the right. Best for first-principles proofs, induction arguments, and any chain where every step needs to be defensible.
derivation.sample:
<!-- _class: math derivation -->

## Derivative of $f$ from first principles.

| Step                                                     | Justification             |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------- |
| $f(x+h) = f(x) + f'(x)\,h + O(h^2)$                      | Taylor expansion, $n = 2$ |
| $f(x+h) - f(x) = f'(x)\,h + O(h^2)$                      | subtract $f(x)$           |
| $\dfrac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h} = f'(x) + O(h)$                  | divide by $h \neq 0$      |
| $\displaystyle\lim_{h\to 0} \dfrac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h} = f'(x)$ | take the limit            |

theorem.label: Theorem тАФ Definition / Theorem / Proof cards
theorem.caption: Stacked colour-coded blockquote cards in the formal-statement vocabulary mathematicians expect тАФ Definition, Theorem, Lemma, Proof. The card boundary preserves the convention that each formal statement is its own atom.
theorem.sample:
<!-- _class: math theorem -->

## Intermediate Value Theorem.

> **Definition.** A function $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is *continuous* on $[a,b]$ if $\lim_{x\to c} f(x) = f(c)$ for every $c \in [a,b]$.

> **Theorem.** Let $f$ be continuous on $[a,b]$ and let $y$ lie strictly between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$. Then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ with $f(c) = y$.

> **Proof.** Set $S = \{x \in [a,b] : f(x) < y\}$. $S$ is non-empty and bounded; let $c = \sup S$. Continuity at $c$ forces $f(c) = y$. $\square$

compare.label: Compare тАФ two- or three-column equations
compare.caption: Side-by-side equation comparison. Each column has its own h3 label, display equation, and one-line gloss. Best for frequentist-vs-Bayesian, batch-vs-streaming, primal-vs-dual.
compare.sample:
<!-- _class: math compare -->

## Frequentist vs Bayesian point estimate.

### Frequentist

$$ \hat\theta_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_\theta\, p(y \mid \theta) $$

Maximises the likelihood тАФ no prior. Uncertainty quantified by the sampling distribution of $\hat\theta$ across hypothetical repeats.

### Bayesian

$$ \hat\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_\theta\, p(\theta \mid y) $$

Maximises the posterior тАФ conditions on the prior $p(\theta)$. Uncertainty is the posterior itself, no repeated sampling required.

canvas.label: Canvas тАФ equation left, plot right
canvas.caption: Pairs a hero equation with a `functionplot` graph (rendered by the function-plot library). Best when the shape of the function is half the argument тАФ sigmoids, distributions, decision boundaries.
canvas.sample:
<!-- _class: math canvas -->

## The sigmoid.

$$ \sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}} $$

Maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0,1)$. $S$-shaped, $\sigma(0) = 0.5$, steepest slope at the origin.

```functionplot
{
  "data": [
    { "fn": "1 / (1 + exp(-x))" },
    { "fn": "tanh(x)" }
  ],
  "xAxis": { "domain": [-6, 6], "label": "x" },
  "yAxis": { "domain": [-1.1, 1.1], "label": "f(x)" },
  "grid": true
}
```

matrix.label: Matrix тАФ matrix + properties
matrix.caption: Hero matrix with a properties / dimensions / interpretation legend. Best for design matrices, transition matrices, covariance matrices тАФ anywhere the structure of the matrix matters as much as its values.
matrix.sample:
<!-- _class: math matrix -->

## The design matrix $X$.

$$
X = \begin{pmatrix}
1 & x_{11} & \cdots & x_{1p} \\
1 & x_{21} & \cdots & x_{2p} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
1 & x_{n1} & \cdots & x_{np}
\end{pmatrix}
$$

- **shape** тАФ $n \times (p+1)$
- **rows** тАФ observations
- **cols** тАФ intercept + $p$ features
- **rank** тАФ full-rank for OLS to have a unique solution
- **column 0** тАФ all-ones, absorbs the intercept

stats.label: Stats тАФ estimate ┬▒ uncertainty
stats.caption: Point estimate with uncertainty (CI, $p$-value, $n$) followed by plain-English interpretation. Best for treatment-effect slides, regression coefficients, A/B test readouts.
stats.sample:
<!-- _class: math stats -->

## Effect of the treatment.

$$ \hat\beta = 0.42 \pm 0.03 $$

> 95% CI: $[0.36,\; 0.48]$
> $p < 0.001 \quad\cdot\quad n = 1{,}204$

For every additional unit of exposure, the outcome rises by 0.42 SD тАФ roughly an **8%** shift on the baseline. Effect size is the headline; the $p$-value just rules out chance.

decompose.label: Matrix ┬╖ decompose тАФ factorisation sequence
decompose.caption: A compound of `matrix`: lays a factorisation out as a sequence of matrices (LU, QR, SVD). Author it as `math matrix decompose` with the product written out. Use when the structure of the decomposition is the point.
decompose.sample:
<!-- _class: math matrix decompose -->

## LU decomposition.

$$
\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

- **$A$** тАФ the original matrix being factorised
- **$L$** тАФ lower-triangular, unit diagonal
- **$U$** тАФ upper-triangular
- **use** тАФ solve $Ax = b$ by forward then back substitution
